sound_type: deep grunts + wing beat -- low guttural grunts and moaning calls from burrow entrances and colony perches (200-800 Hz); rapid whirring wingbeat sound at cliff-edge launch (mechanical, not vocalization); ambient colony texture of dozens of grunting pairs and launching birds
ecosystem: Rocky island -- steep-sided rocky islands and headlands of the outer Pacific coast; soil burrow nesting on island tops; cliff-edge launching behavior; Destruction Island, Tatoosh Island (Cape Flattery), Quillayute Needles (Olympic Peninsula), Three Arch Rocks NWR (Oregon)
season: Summer (colony occupancy late April through mid-August; peak acoustic June-July during incubation and early chick-rearing)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN globally). Pacific Northwest populations showing concerning decline trends: some major colony sites reduced significantly. Climate change prey availability shifts and introduced predators at island colonies hypothesized drivers. Of conservation concern in Pacific Northwest.
milestone: FIRST ALCIDAE in SPS engine at degree 294. The auk family enters at its most charismatic Pacific Northwest representative. Acoustic transition: from Laridae sharp-piercing (Common Tern keerr: 2,000-4,000 Hz) to Alcidae deep-guttural (puffin grunt: 200-800 Hz). Two-octave frequency drop.

coordinates.description: Tatoosh Island (Cape Flattery, WA), Destruction Island (Jefferson County WA), Quillayute Needles (Olympic Peninsula), Three Arch Rocks NWR (Oregon coast). Pelagic off-season: continental slope and offshore Pacific, September-April.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Grunting calls and moaning from burrow entrances. Contact calls within pair bond and between colony members. Soft purring to growling quality.
acoustic_data.grunt_call: Low, guttural grunt or moan. Approximately 200-800 Hz fundamental -- lowest frequency of any Laridae/Alcidae entry in engine. Quality: soft growling or purring, not sharp or piercing.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 200-800 Hz. Fundamentally different acoustic register from all preceding Laridae entries (Common Tern 2,000-4,000 Hz; Caspian Tern 800-2,000 Hz; gulls 500-4,000 Hz). A two-octave drop from tern colony screaming to puffin colony grunting.
acoustic_data.wingbeat_sound: Audible at close range. Rapid whirring thwap-thwap-thwap at cliff-edge launch: alcid wings too small for body mass, compensating with frequency. Mechanical sound, not vocalization. Colony soundscape component.
acoustic_data.e6_calibration: E=6 as deep texture: present and substantial but operating at low frequency and ambient rather than sharp-alarm register. You feel the puffin colony as much as you hear it.

nodes.title: First Alcidae in SPS Engine -- Auk Family Enters
nodes.description: The Tufted Puffin at degree 294 is the SPS engine's first Alcidae entry. The auk family enters at its most visually iconic Pacific Northwest representative. The acoustic transition from Laridae (289-293) to Alcidae (294+) is a two-octave frequency shift: from tern keerr (2,000-4,000 Hz) to puffin grunt (200-800 Hz). Future alcid entries calibrated against this first appearance.

nodes.title: Laridae to Alcidae Acoustic Transition
nodes.description: The acoustic shift at degree 293-294 boundary is the engine's most dramatic frequency transition in the coastal seabird arc: from Caspian Tern ka-arr (800-2,000 Hz) to Tufted Puffin grunt (200-800 Hz). Sharp-piercing to deep-guttural. The taxonomy is audible.

nodes.title: Fratercula -- The Puffin Genus
nodes.description: Three species: Tufted Puffin (Pacific, largest), Atlantic Puffin (Atlantic, most iconic), Horned Puffin (North Pacific). All share: compressed colored bill sheath shed in winter, white breeding face (except Tufted in winter), black upperparts, burrow nesting, single-egg clutch. Fratercula: Latin for 'little brother' from friar's-robe color suggestion.

nodes.title: Pacific Northwest Tufted Puffin Decline
nodes.description: PNW Tufted Puffin populations showing concerning declines. Some major colony sites substantially reduced. Climate change effects on prey availability, introduced predators at island colonies, ocean productivity shifts hypothesized drivers. Global population stable; PNW population a regional conservation concern.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=6 = E=13. Two firsts at degree 294: THE proto-grunge origin band (Green River) and FIRST ALCIDAE (Tufted Puffin). Both establishing firsts: Sub Pop's origin and the auk family's origin in the engine.
Last Laridae (postscript). Ka-arr at 800-2,000 Hz. Tufted Puffin grunt at 200-800 Hz: the frequency transition into Alcidae is the acoustic boundary between the two families.
Second Alcidae. Consecutive entries. Tufted Puffin diurnal (day); Rhinoceros Auklet nocturnal (night). Together they document the full temporal range of the outer coast alcid colony.
